me.auction_style: Analytics-driven and highly active traders who approach the auction with detailed preparation, including custom valuation tools. They favor a balanced roster construction with smart value hunting rather than pure stars-and-scrubs, though they will go deep on elite players at premium prices. Terry Lyons has been primary draft-night GM; Craig handles analysis and in-season management.
me.price_tendency_detail: Chathams are methodical valuers who build custom auction tools — they are unlikely to overbid on emotion but will pay up for scarce position value. Their trades suggest they understand marginal value well and negotiate deliberately.
me.trade_style: Very active traders. Chathams are the most trade-active team in the league by Barry Carlin's own description ('sixth trade of the season' callouts). They reach out proactively, offer multiple packages, and iterate quickly. They are known for roster churning — 2023 championship description was 'masters of roster turnover'. They favor pitching-for-hitting swaps and are willing to eat injury risk for upside.
notable_tendencies: Most trade-active team in league history; Barry explicitly notes their volume each season
Builds custom valuation tools and auction software (this very app) — thoroughly prepared at draft
Terry Lyons is the senior partner; Craig Glaser handles analytics and in-season moves
Will take on injury risk: traded Alec Bohm for Brandon Nimmo, Yandy Diaz/Williams/Megill for Zebby Matthews/Reynolds/Realmuto
Won 2022 and 2023 championships back-to-back plus 2019 co-title; most recent dynasty in the league
Craig expressed uncertainty about 2026 commitment early in the cycle — Terry is the driving force
me.intel_summary: Chathams are the analytics arm of this league — Craig builds the tools and Terry brings the competitive fire. They win through relentless in-season activity and excellent draft prep, not by overpaying at auction. Their greatest vulnerability is that Craig's engagement can be inconsistent, and they sometimes hold underperforming players too long mid-season before finally pulling the trigger on a trade.

t1.auction_style: Spew are experienced, opportunistic drafters who emphasize speed/steals and hitting depth. They are willing to make big trades (acquired Pedro Strop/Torii Hunter block, gave away Arenado in a 2013 deal) and have shown a preference for balanced rosters over stars-and-scrubs. Andrew Lombardi handles most day-to-day communication; Brian Garber is quieter but strategically engaged. They are serial champions (2003, 2004, 2013, 2020, 2021, 2025) suggesting consistently strong draft execution.
t1.price_tendency_detail: No direct auction price data. Historical evidence suggests they get value at OF and RP while not overspending on starting pitching — Lombardi has repeatedly and vocally complained that Wins is a bad category, suggesting they deprioritize win-heavy SPs.
t1.trade_style: Moderately active traders. They make targeted moves to shore up category needs (speed, batting average). In 2013 they gave away young Arenado for veterans and won the title, suggesting they value proven contributors over prospects. Lombardi is vocal in trades — he pushed back hard on a 2019 Chathams offer, accusing them of poor form, showing he negotiates from conviction.
notable_tendencies: Andrew Lombardi is the vocal personality — quick-witted, self-deprecating humor, handles all league communication
Brian Garber is the quieter strategist, based in Arizona; team has a bi-coastal dynamic
Loudest critics of the Wins category — Lombardi has repeatedly pushed for Quality Starts as a replacement
6 championships (most in league history) — consistent performers across different eras
Lombardi also handles league finances as treasurer — positions him as a central league figure with extra intel
In 2013 they famously traded Nolan Arenado + Dempster + Moreland for Strop/Hunter/Rogers and won the title
t1.intel_summary: Spew are the winningest franchise in Teddy Ballgame history, and they know it. Lombardi's finance role gives him information advantages on who has paid and who is distracted. They are not sentimental about young players and will deal prospects for proven pieces. Expect them to be aggressive early in the auction on OF depth and late-round value; they tend to underinvest in starting pitching and compensate with waiver pickups.

t2.auction_style: Joe Achille is a veteran champion (4 titles: 2007, 2009, 2011, 2016) who runs the auction Zoom logistics for the league — giving him detailed familiarity with the live auction environment. Based on his trade negotiations, he is assertive about fair value and willing to offer multiple SP in deals, suggesting he drafts pitching depth. His challenge style in the 2019 Chathams trade negotiations — where he aggressively called out Craig for poor trade form — shows he takes negotiations seriously and won't quietly accept lowball offers.
t2.price_tendency_detail: Insufficient auction price data. His 2019 trade behavior — offering multiple SPs and speed while demanding specific outfielders — suggests he builds surplus pitching at auction and uses it as trade currency.
t2.trade_style: Active and sometimes confrontational. Achille initiates trades by reading the 'On the Block' postings and constructs multi-player offers. He becomes frustrated when counterparts don't engage meaningfully and will call it out directly. He is willing to negotiate and modify proposals but expects genuine dialogue in return.
notable_tendencies: Runs the Zoom logistics for draft night every year — has seen every auction live from the moderator seat
Gets genuinely frustrated by non-responsive trade partners and says so directly via iPhone messages
Has won championships in 2007, 2009, 2011, and 2016 — consistent long-term performer
Noted by league as a fellow Red Sox fan alongside Korbak — possible drafting affinity for Boston players
In 2017 sarcastically noted he'd have the '4th honor' of nominating first as defending champ, then deflated
Willing to package multiple SPs to acquire specific position needs (speed, OF depth)
t2.intel_summary: Achille is the league's most experienced champion and a formidable auction competitor. He runs the Zoom so he watches every bid carefully and knows how people bid under pressure. His confrontational trade style suggests he has strong conviction on player values and won't be pushed around. Watch for him to build surplus SP depth at the auction and flip it for hitting needs later in the season.

t3.auction_style: Barry is the Commissioner — he sends the vast majority of emails and controls league operations. His email threads show he's organized, detail-oriented, and runs a tight ship. As a competitor, he's a three-time champion (2006, 2015, 2017) suggesting he's skilled at auction prep. His commissioner role also gives him unique intel on all transactions, injuries, and rule interpretations before others. He tends toward balanced, disciplined roster building based on his approach to league administration.
t3.price_tendency_detail: As Commissioner, Barry rarely discusses his own team's strategy openly. His detailed knowledge of league rules and procedures suggests he exploits edge cases and roster flexibility better than most.
t3.trade_style: Selectively active. Barry's commissioner role means he processes all trades, giving him full information. He occasionally makes targeted moves but appears more conservative than teams like Chathams or Let's Deal. His 2023 team of the week win with Devers/Peralta suggests he values elite power hitters and top-line SP.
notable_tendencies: Commissioner since founding — processes all trades and has full information on every transaction in real time
3 championships (2006, 2015, 2017) — proves he competes seriously despite administrative load
Writes annual season-in-review recaps — knows every team's moves and results in exhaustive detail
Uses humor in emails: 'As a team owner I hope everyone is unprepared!' etc.
Son Derek Carlin co-owns Velvet Thunder (t10) — potential coordination concern
Voted to keep Team of the Week bonus (which he won Week 21 in 2023) vs. Team of the Month proposal
t3.intel_summary: Barry is the most informed person in the league by virtue of his commissioner role — he sees every trade, every pickup, every injury replacement request. He competes seriously and has won three titles, though his administrative duties may distract from in-season optimization. His son's team creates a potential conflict of interest to be aware of during trade negotiations. His 105-thread email history is almost entirely administrative broadcasts, making his personal draft strategy hard to read.

t4.auction_style: No data available. Thread file does not exist.
t4.price_tendency_detail: No thread file exists for t4.
notable_tendencies: No thread file (threads_t4.json) was found in the owner_profiles directory
Owner slot may not be filled or data was not collected
t4.intel_summary: No data available for t4. This owner slot either does not exist or email threads were not collected.

t5.auction_style: Andrew Grossman is methodical and somewhat old-school — he's the one who still sends checks and references Strat-O-Matic. He's a die-hard Mets fan which historically colored his player preferences. The Widowmakers are a 2-time championship team (2010, 2019 co-champ) suggesting solid auction fundamentals. Andrew typically speaks for both himself and Susan on team decisions, and tends toward conservative, balanced roster construction.
t5.price_tendency_detail: No direct price data. His trade behavior — asking for Lance Lynn in exchange for a quality OF — suggests he understands market value but may be conservative on closer/RP spending.
t5.trade_style: Selectively active. The Grossmans will engage in trades when the deal is clearly explained. His own trade asking for Lance Lynn for an OF suggests he values SP-for-OF swaps. He confirmed trades immediately which shows he's responsive when engaged. Signs off as 'Da Widowmakers' — committed team identity.
notable_tendencies: Andrew is a die-hard Mets fan — historically may have had NY player bias at auction
Still sends paper checks to Lombardi — old-school approach extends to draft philosophy
Mets fan self-declared: 'I for one am a die-hard Mets fan and invite you to take that into consideration'
Signs emails as 'Baseball Widowmakers' or 'Da Widowmakers' — committed to league identity
2022: asked for Lance Lynn in exchange for non-Alvarez/non-Harper OF — values aces at premium
Congratulated Chathams: 'You made us dizzy with all of those moves' — not a high-churn team himself
t5.intel_summary: The Grossmans are a steady, experienced team that competes without flash. Andrew's Mets fandom and old-school sensibilities suggest he may slightly overpay for likable/familiar players. He's not going to out-hustle you on waivers but he'll build a solid auction roster and hold firm in trade negotiations. Worth probing for SP trades mid-season — he seems to see pitching as a trade chip.

t6.auction_style: Andy Korbak is a calculated, patient strategist — the 2018 champion shows he can build a winning team. He's a Red Sox fan like Achille. His trade approach reveals he prefers big, impactful deals over small moves: 'Looking a bigger deal, basically a top SP, for a top bat.' He checks league rules carefully before the draft and seems methodical. He tends to want top-end value at his positions of need.
t6.price_tendency_detail: No direct auction price data. Trade behavior suggests he values elite bats at a premium — he declined to move Mookie Betts after just acquiring him and sought 'a top SP for a top bat' in negotiations, suggesting he prices stars correctly.
t6.trade_style: Selective and deliberate. Korbak will put players on the block but takes his time responding (often traveling, on vacation). He declines to move his core players after recently acquiring them. He engages thoughtfully but won't be rushed.
notable_tendencies: Red Sox fan — may have hometown bias at auction alongside Achille
Took a family vacation 3/21-3/29 in 2026 — scheduling constraint that pushed draft date
Consistently jokes about Chathams' excessive roster moves with good humor
Prefers large trades to small ones — explicitly said he was 'looking for a bigger deal' when Chathams offered a modest swap
Won 2018 title — proves capable of top-end results when he puts it together
Checks league rules carefully pre-draft — thorough preparer
t6.intel_summary: Korbak is a patient, well-prepared competitor who won't panic-trade his best players. He builds for the big deal and is selective about what he moves. His Red Sox allegiance is worth noting at the auction. He tends to travel or be unavailable during key windows — if you want to trade with him, initiate early.

t7.auction_style: Alex Tarshis is the most active and analytically inclined trader in the league, having won the 2024 championship. He engages immediately with trade talk ('open for business!'), references auction context in negotiations ('you guys were in on him at the auction'), and seeks value gaps in player comparisons. He's a balanced builder who prioritizes steals/speed and specific positional needs. He names his team 'Let's Deal' — the branding is a philosophy.
t7.price_tendency_detail: Tarshis understands relative value well — he explicitly noted 'I'm not sure there is a significant value gap between Nimmo and Lowe seeing as Nimmo plays the more premium position.' He uses position scarcity as a pricing argument and won't overpay for positionally fungible players.
t7.trade_style: Very active and sophisticated. Tarshis initiates trades frequently, engages every offer, and provides clear reasoning for his positions. He's comfortable in both buyer and seller roles and uses tier-based language showing he thinks systematically. He also fishes with low offers to 'start a dialogue' then negotiates toward fair value.
notable_tendencies: Named his team 'Let's Deal' — genuinely lives up to it with consistently high trade volume
2024 league champion — peak performer, likely entered 2026 as the team others want to dethrone
References historical auction prices in trade talks — tracks what players cost at the draft
Thinks in tiers: 'two 7s for a 9 type offer' — sophisticated relative value framing
Speed/SB is a stated priority: 'wouldn't trade any of my guys projected for 15 steals' for Lowe
Joked about saving $13 by saying he 'vehemently disagrees' — has a dry sense of humor
t7.intel_summary: Tarshis is the most sophisticated trader in the league and the reigning champion. He will engage with almost any trade proposal but knows what he wants and prices position scarcity correctly. His focus on speed/SB contributors is a clear drafting signal — expect him to overbid on fast OF with position flexibility. He is harder to fleece than any other owner in the league.

t8.auction_style: Bryan Boardman is a casual-but-engaged competitor who builds OF-heavy rosters and then tries to flip his outfield surplus for pitching or positional needs. He goes by 'Boardy' in league parlance. He's enthusiastic at the start of negotiations but frequently pulls back after initially showing interest. He anchors high on his own stars but eventually comes around to reasonable deals.
t8.price_tendency_detail: Boardman consistently overvalues his own players in trade asks — offered Kershaw for Judge, claimed Cole + Realmuto needed more than Arenado + McMahon, asked for Ohtani as a starting point for Hader. He anchors very high but ultimately does fair deals.
t8.trade_style: Active initiator but slow follow-through. Boardman puts players 'on the block' frequently and invites trade talk, but then backs away when the season is going well or when he's busy with work. He has done extensive trading with Chathams across many years and is a comfortable, reliable partner once a deal framework is agreed.
notable_tendencies: Historically OF-rich at auction — frequently offers outfield surplus and hunts for pitching or catchers
Consistently overvalues his core assets in initial asks then comes down to fair deals
Works at the NBA — goes dark during NBA crunch periods (playoffs, bubble, trade deadline)
Voted himself 'team of the week' for getting the Korea Opening Series games — competitive and self-aware
B Squared era trades: dealt Kershaw for Judge (2018) and Moore for Melky (2016), showing he's not afraid of name trades
One-word trade confirmations ('confirmed', 'approved', 'good') — brief communicator
t8.intel_summary: Boardman is an OF hoarder who will almost always have surplus outfield by midseason. He's willing to trade but tends to anchor high on his best players — get him engaged early and let him come to you on price. His NBA job means he periodically disappears during the season; if he goes quiet it may be timing, not disinterest. He and Chathams have a comfortable multi-year trading relationship.

t9.auction_style: The Diamond Hacks are the expansion team that joined in 2022, recruited by Brian Garber. Andy Enzweiler is in graphic design (Arizona) and Ed O'Brien is an attorney (Tempe, AZ). Ed explicitly stated he values bullpen very little — 'Diamondbacks-White Sox fan so bullpen-agnostic, historically' — suggesting RP is deprioritized at auction. Ed also revealed he made a catastrophic trade in a previous league (Semien for Plesac in Month 1), meaning Ed hands off all trades to Andy.
t9.price_tendency_detail: Only 3 threads available, none with direct auction pricing data. Ed's stated bullpen-agnostic philosophy and expansion team status suggests they may underspend on closers and relievers.
t9.trade_style: Unknown from limited data. Ed explicitly said Andy handles all trades after his own disastrous trade history. The team is enthusiastic and social in group emails but no direct trade negotiations are captured.
notable_tendencies: Ed O'Brien is the league's comedian — Barry called him 'the Chris Rock of the Teddy Ballgame League'
Ed handles no trades: 'my co-manager will handle all trades here' — Andy is the trading partner
Ed is explicitly bullpen-agnostic: 'Diamondbacks-White Sox fan so bullpen-agnostic, historically'
Expansion team since 2022 — still developing pattern recognition within this specific league
Ed's previous league: sold 'never gonna last' Semien for Plesac in Month 1 — self-aware about bad decisions
Based in Tempe, AZ — in Mountain Time, 3 hours behind draft start; may be disadvantaged in live auction pace
t9.intel_summary: The Diamond Hacks are a fun team that has yet to challenge for a title. Ed's bullpen-agnostic philosophy is a clear exploitable signal — they likely underspend on closers and RPs, which could create waiver/trade opportunities. Andy handles all in-season moves so direct your trade outreach to him. As a team still learning the league's rhythms, they may make mispriced nominations at the auction that benefit the table.

t10.auction_style: No email threads available. Derek Carlin is Barry Carlin's son, which is the primary intel on this team. Barry referenced Derek losing internet access during the 2025 practice draft, suggesting they sometimes draft together from the same location. Being a next-generation owner, Derek likely plays differently than the league veterans.
notable_tendencies: Derek is Barry Carlin's son — potential coordination with the Commissioner's team warrants awareness
Zero email threads collected — either doesn't communicate via the league email chain or data was not captured
In 2025 practice draft, Barry and Derek lost internet together suggesting they may draft from the same location
Justin Hurson is co-owner — no individual email presence in the data
t10.intel_summary: Velvet Thunder is a data black hole. Zero thread history means no patterns to analyze. The key fact is that Derek is Barry's son, the Commissioner. Whether or not this creates actual informational advantages is impossible to assess from available data, but it's worth noting. Treat this team as an unknown quantity — prepare for any style.
